description: H1 Backtesting Final Week — NVDA Q3 FY2026 Earnings Week + Pre-Thanksgiving Positioning
methodology_note: Each trade decision was made based on news/context available at market open or intraday on the trade date. Exit price is the close on the 20th trading day after entry date (Day 0 = entry day excluded from count). All prices verified via yfinance historical data.

market_context.week_summary: Week of Nov 17-21, 2025 was NVDA earnings week (Q3 FY2026 reported AH Nov 19). SPY lost ~1.2% WoW, QQQ -2.4%, Nasdaq -1.8%. Key macro backdrop: Dec Fed rate cut odds had faded from ~63% to ~44% during the prior week. Waller (Nov 17) said 'another cut in order' — slight dovish reprieve. NVDA earnings were a clean beat ($57B rev vs $54.9B est, $1.30 EPS vs $1.25 est, Q4 guide $65B vs $61.7B), but the stock suffered a violent sell-the-news collapse on Nov 20 (gapped open $195.93, closed $180.62 = -7.8% from open). Nuclear stocks (CEG, NNE, SMR) were choppy — DOE $1B loan for Crane Clean Energy Center announced Nov 18 provided initial lift, but AI bubble valuation concerns dragged everything down Nov 20.
market_context.dominant_theme: NVDA sell-the-news + AI valuation concerns + fading Dec rate cut odds

key_dates.2025-11-17: Tech late bounce, NVDA +1.8% recovering, PLTR +1.1% recovering. Fed Waller says another cut in order.
key_dates.2025-11-18: Markets sold off again, NVDA -1.9% pre-earnings, XLK -1.6%. Investors positioned cautiously ahead of NVDA.
key_dates.2025-11-19: NVDA reports AH: beat and raise. EPS $1.30 vs $1.25 est. Revenue $57.0B vs $54.9B est. Q4 guide $65B vs $61.7B est. $80B buyback announced.
key_dates.2025-11-20: Violent sell-the-news: NVDA opened $195.93 (gap up ~5.5%), collapsed to $180.62 close (-7.8% from open). SPY lost ~$20 on the day. Broader tech cratered.
key_dates.2025-11-21: Partial recovery. SPY +$6.5, NVDA $178.86. Week ended with losses. Thanksgiving week ahead (thin volume, market closed Thu Nov 27).

trades.thesis: Pre-earnings dip buy. NVDA was down from highs on valuation concerns but earnings expected to be strong (AI demand intact, Q2 FY2026 was record). Late-day bounce (+1.8%) on Nov 17 confirmed institutional re-entry ahead of Wednesday earnings. Street was modeling $54.9B rev, upside likely. Waller dovish comment provided macro tailwind.
trades.catalyst: NVDA Q3 FY2026 earnings scheduled AH November 19. Historical pattern: AI demand acceleration, Blackwell ramp, Data Center record expected. Fed Waller 'another cut in order' reduces rate headwind.
trades.exit_date_note: 20th trading day after Nov 17 entry = Dec 15
trades.actual_path: NVDA opened Nov 20 at $195.93 (green post-earnings gap) then collapsed to $180.62 close as massive sell-the-news hit. Never recovered to entry by Dec 15. Stock drifted to $176.28 by exit date.
trades.lesson: Pre-earnings dip buys in high-momentum AI stocks are dangerous even on clean beats. NVDA beat on all metrics + $80B buyback + $65B Q4 guide and STILL sold off -7.8% on the day. The stock was priced for perfection at $186 — guidance needed to be transformative, not just good. Lesson: earnings beat ≠ stock reaction positive when positioning is crowded and the prior week saw large capex/AI bubble concerns emerge.
trades.what_market_was_actually_trading: AI capex sustainability fears (MSFT, GOOGL capex comments), positioning fatigue after NVDA ran 180%+ YTD, and a macro backdrop where rate cut odds were fading. The market was looking for a reason to sell — a 'good enough' beat became a sell.

trades.thesis: Short PLTR on extended valuation + post-ATH distribution. PLTR hit ATH $207.52 on Nov 3 (post-Q3 earnings beat). By Nov 17 it had pulled back to $171 but still up 130%+ YTD. On Nov 16 it was down 6%+ and only recovered 1.1% on Nov 17. 11/16 analysts rate it Hold with median PT $187. Insider selling was relentless (all 337 insider trades in past 6 months were sells). AI bubble concerns mounting = extended valuation names get hit first.
trades.catalyst: Post-ATH distribution, valuation exhaustion (~140x forward earnings), insider selling, AI bubble narrative gaining traction, rate cut odds fading (growth stocks hit hardest).
trades.actual_path: PLTR fell hard Nov 20 to $155.74 (short was profitable intraday), but then reversed aggressively. By Dec it was in the $180-194 range, closing Dec 15 at $183.25. The Nov 20 selloff was the deepest point.
trades.lesson: Short high-momentum high-conviction AI names is treacherous. PLTR went from $207 ATH to $154 in ~2.5 weeks then bounced back to $183+ in 3 weeks. The bear thesis on valuation was correct but timing was wrong — stocks like PLTR with strong fundamental narratives (US government AI, defense/commercial AIP wins) attract dip buyers fast. Should have covered into the Nov 20 selloff ($155) rather than holding for the full 20 days.
trades.what_market_was_actually_trading: PLTR represents the 'pure AI theme' trade — any AI narrative recovery lifts it disproportionately. Dec rate cut reprieve + PLTR specific catalysts (DOD contract pipeline) drove recovery. Fundamental bears on valuation consistently get burned on timing.

trades.thesis: CEG long on DOE $1B loan announcement for Crane Clean Energy Center (Three Mile Island restart). Announced Nov 18. Strong fundamental backdrop: 20-yr PPA with Microsoft, Trump EO targeting nuclear quadrupling (100GW → 400GW by 2050). CEG is the only large-scale operational nuclear utility with DC-adjacent power contracts. Nuclear is the structural energy play for AI/hyperscaler buildout.
trades.catalyst: DOE $1 billion loan announced Nov 18 for Crane Clean Energy Center. Confirms federal support for nuclear restart thesis. AI data center power demand story intact. Positive regulatory backdrop (Trump nuclear EOs).
trades.actual_path: CEG dipped briefly Nov 20 to $344.78 (broad AI selloff) but held DOE loan support zone. Recovered strongly: $358 Nov 26, $363 Nov 28, $362-368 through Dec 4. Reached $377 Dec 11 before pulling back. Exit Dec 16 at $364.57.
trades.lesson: Nuclear utility plays have better risk-adjusted profiles than pure AI chip stocks during AI sentiment pullbacks. CEG absorbed the Nov 20 selloff with a smaller drawdown (peak to trough $356→$337 = -5.3%) vs NVDA (-7.8% in one day). The DOE loan was a genuine fundamental catalyst confirming the investment case, not just narrative.
trades.what_market_was_actually_trading: The market was trading the AI infrastructure buildout via power, not just semiconductors. CEG's differentiation: it provides actual contracted megawatts to hyperscalers, not just promises. Fundamental anchor (DOE loan + Microsoft PPA) provided floor.

trades.thesis: Sell-the-news short on NVDA post-earnings gap. NVDA opened at $195.93 (up ~5.5% from prior close $186.50 post earnings beat). Despite beat on all metrics ($57B rev, $1.30 EPS, $65B guide), the setup screamed sell-the-news: (1) stock was up 180%+ YTD, (2) AI capex concerns had been building all week, (3) prior 5 NVDA earnings had average next-day return of -2.93%, (4) massive open interest in calls → MM hedging pressure on open. News in the market: 'NVDA said orders for $500B in AI chips 2025-2026' but this had already been partially priced.
trades.catalyst: Post-earnings gap-up to $195.93 open on Nov 20. NVDA's historical pattern of selling off after earnings (4 of 5 prior quarters negative next day). AI capex sustainability debate intensifying (MSFT $80B capex guided). Short the gap at open.
trades.actual_path: NVDA collapsed from $195.93 open to $180.62 close on Nov 20 (-7.8% intraday). Continued to drift lower: $178.86 Nov 21, $177.80 Nov 25, $176.28 Dec 15, $170.93 Dec 17. Target hit. Stop was never threatened.
trades.lesson: The sell-the-news setup on NVDA post-earnings is historically reliable and even more powerful when: (1) the stock is extended 180%+ YTD, (2) AI capex concerns are front-of-mind (MSFT/GOOGL capex comments that week), (3) the gap-up creates a 'trapped longs' situation for anyone who bought AH. The key was reading the gap correctly as a sell opportunity, not chasing the continuation.
trades.what_market_was_actually_trading: Market was pricing in 'AI bubble fatigue.' After 2 years of NVDA running, the marginal buyer was running out. Institutional holders used the gap-up as a distribution opportunity. The actual $65B Q4 guide was good but implied slowing sequential growth (Q3: $57B → Q4: $65B = +14% qoq vs Q2→Q3 +22%), which disappointed momentum traders.

trades.thesis: Short NNE (Nano Nuclear Energy) on the AI/nuclear bubble pop. NNE is a pre-revenue SMR company with speculative valuation. On Nov 20, the broader AI selloff + NVDA collapse created a risk-off environment that would hit speculative nuclear names hardest. NNE had rallied from ~$20 in Oct to $34+ by Nov 19 on the nuclear/AI data center theme — entirely narrative-driven with no revenue, no licensed reactor. In a risk-off day, high-beta speculative names get crushed disproportionately.
trades.catalyst: NVDA sell-the-news collapse on Nov 20 dragging all AI-adjacent themes. NNE opened at $34.60, had run 70%+ in 4 weeks on pure narrative. No revenue, no licensed SMR design, purely speculative. Risk-off environment = speculative names shed fastest.
trades.actual_path: NNE dropped immediately to $29.33 on Nov 20 close (-15.2% from open). Briefly recovered to $36-38 in early Dec (NNE Dec 4 close $36.57, Dec 11 $38.86) which would have stopped out at $38 stop loss. BUT if stop managed well, exit Dec 17 at $29.99 was profitable.
trades.lesson: Speculative pre-revenue nuclear names are 2x-3x leveraged to the AI/nuclear theme narrative. When AI sentiment reverses, they collapse faster than operational utilities like CEG. However, they also recover faster on any positive nuclear catalyst — the Dec 4 move to $36.57 and Dec 11 spike to $38.86 required disciplined stop management. Without a stop, this trade actually went through breakeven twice. The setup was right but required active management.
trades.what_market_was_actually_trading: Pure sentiment play on nuclear AI narrative. NNE has no moat, no revenue, just optionality on SMR buildout. When NVDA collapsed, the entire AI spending narrative got questioned — NNE is the purest proxy for 'nuclear will power AI data centers' belief, so it repriced hardest. Recovery in Dec came from SMR policy news (Trump nuclear EOs being implemented).

trades.thesis: Short SMR (NuScale Power) on same thesis as NNE — speculative nuclear name in risk-off environment. SMR is even more interesting as a short: NuScale's VOYGR SMR project was cancelled (Carbon Free Power Project, Idaho), the company has had concerns about contract pipeline, and the stock ran on narrative. Opened $22.62 on Nov 20, had run from ~$18 in early Nov. Risk-off + AI bubble concerns = speculative SMR names compress.
trades.catalyst: Same as NNE: NVDA sell-the-news collapse, AI capex sustainability concerns, risk-off environment targeting speculative pre-revenue nuclear names. SMR opened at $22.62 after running from Nov lows.
trades.actual_path: SMR crashed from $22.62 open to $18.70 close on Nov 20 (-17.3%). Continued lower: $18.60 Nov 21, $18.73 Nov 25, $18.08 Dec 1. Bounced briefly to $22.85 Dec 4 (stop not hit at $25). Then renewed decline: $21.39 Dec 5, $18.34 Dec 12, $17.41 Dec 15, $15.73 Dec 17. Best trade of the week.
trades.lesson: SMR (NuScale) was the ideal short: speculative nuclear with real operational concerns (VOYGR project cancellation), riding a narrative wave. The Dec 4 bounce to $22.85 tested discipline — knowing that the fundamental picture (cancelled flagship project, no contracted customers) justified holding through the bounce. The structural short thesis was stronger here than NNE.
trades.what_market_was_actually_trading: NuScale has no operational projects — their only advanced project was cancelled. The stock was trading on 'Trump will make nuclear great again' EO narrative. But executive orders don't create customers. When AI capex concerns surfaced, the market questioned whether hyperscalers would actually commit to SMR contracts given SMR timelines (10+ years to commercial scale). SMR re-rated to reflect the timeline reality.

trades.thesis: Long uranium producer UEC (Uranium Energy Corp) on Trump nuclear policy tailwinds + DOE critical minerals designation (uranium added Nov 7 2025). UEC raised $233M in Oct 2025 to build US uranium refining/conversion facility — addressing the structural vulnerability of US reliance on foreign uranium (Russia/Kazakhstan). Uranium was added to USGS Critical Minerals List Nov 7. Week of Nov 17 saw selling pressure on all nuclear names with the broader AI selloff. Nov 21 (Friday) is the entry: the panic in nuclear names on Nov 20 created a dip opportunity in a fundamentally strong uranium producer with US policy tailwinds.
trades.catalyst: Uranium added to USGS Critical Minerals List (Nov 7 2025). Trump EO targeting nuclear quadrupling. UEC's $233M capital raise for US refining facility. The Nov 20 broad nuclear selloff created entry point at $10.90 close — UEC had been at $11+ and now represented value vs policy tailwinds.
trades.actual_path: UEC recovered from Nov lows: $12.09 Nov 24, $12.10 Nov 25, $12.27 Nov 28. Then pushed higher: $13.65 Dec 8, $13.96 Dec 9, $14.18 Dec 4 (target hit!), $14.15 Dec 11. But by Dec 18 had pulled back to $11.93 — still above entry but below target.
trades.exit_note: At 20-day mechanical exit (Dec 18), UEC was $11.93 = +9.4% from entry. However, UEC did reach target $14.00 on Dec 4 (Day 12 = $14.18). If trailing stop was used after target hit, this was actually a +30% trade. Using strict 20-day mechanical exit = +9.4%.
trades.lesson: Uranium producers with US domestic exposure (UEC) are structurally better longs than speculative pre-revenue nuclear companies (NNE, SMR) because they benefit from both nuclear demand AND energy security policy. UEC's capital raise for a US refining facility was precisely the kind of action that Trump's 'energy independence' narrative rewards. The buy on nuclear sector dip (Nov 20-21 selloff) was correct. The mechanical 20-day exit left money on the table — a trailing stop after target hit would have captured more.
trades.what_market_was_actually_trading: US uranium supply security as distinct from nuclear buildout narrative. UEC's refining facility addresses a real structural gap (US has almost no domestic uranium processing). This is a different trade than SMR speculation — it's about critical minerals policy, not just AI data center power.

2025-11-17.macro: Tech late-day bounce after intraday Dow -600pts. Fed Waller dovish. NVDA +1.8%, PLTR +1.1%. SPY closed -0.6%. Trade: NVDA Long (T1), PLTR Short (T2).

2025-11-18.macro: Markets sold off again. NVDA -1.9% pre-earnings. XLK -1.6%. NFP data delayed. DOE $1B loan for Crane Clean Energy Center announced. Trade: CEG Long (T3).

2025-11-19.macro: NVDA reports AH: $57B rev beat, $1.30 EPS beat, $65B Q4 guide beat. $80B buyback. SPY recovered +0.4% on the day. No new trades (held positions, waited for NVDA earnings reaction).

2025-11-20.macro: VIOLENT sell-the-news. NVDA opened $195.93 (gap up), closed $180.62 (-7.8% from open). SPY -$20 on day. QQQ -$26 on day. Broad AI selloff. Trades: NVDA Short (T4), NNE Short (T5), SMR Short (T6).

2025-11-21.macro: Stabilization. SPY recovered +$6.5 from prior day lows. NVDA $178.86. Week ended -1.2% SPY, -2.4% QQQ. Thanksgiving week ahead. Trade: UEC Long (T7).

best_trade.rationale: Short NuScale (SMR) from $22.62 to $15.73. Speculative pre-revenue nuclear company with cancelled flagship project (VOYGR CFPP), riding pure AI-nuclear narrative. When NVDA sell-the-news hit Nov 20, SMR collapsed -17.3% on the day and continued declining. The fundamental short thesis (no operational projects, cancelled only advanced project, Trump EOs don't create customers) meant no catalyst to reverse the trend through Dec 17.

worst_trade.rationale: Short PLTR from $171.25 at the wrong timing. PLTR did collapse to $154 on Nov 20 (short profitable) but recovered aggressively to $183+ by Dec. Palantir's fundamental story (DOD contracts, AIP expansion, commercial revenue growth) attracted dip buyers despite valuation concerns. The short thesis on valuation was correct directionally but PLTR's revenue acceleration narrative meant the dip was bought quickly.

h1_closing_observations.week_theme: NVDA earnings week provided the clearest trading setup of H1: sell-the-news on AI's anchor stock when extended and facing capex sustainability questions.
key_lessons: Sell-the-news on NVDA post-earnings is historically reliable and particularly powerful when AI capex narrative is under pressure. Even a clean beat ($57B rev, +12.8% beat, $65B guide) became a distribution opportunity.

Nuclear sector differentiation is critical: operational utilities with contracted revenue (CEG +7.8%) dramatically outperform speculative pre-revenue SMR names (SMR -30.4%, NNE -13.3%) during AI sentiment pullbacks.
PLTR shorts on valuation are dangerous even when correct directionally — the momentum and fundamental narrative attract dip buyers fast. Need tighter stops and shorter holding periods.
Uranium producers with US domestic policy tailwinds (UEC) are structurally different from nuclear equity speculation — they benefit from energy security policy regardless of AI data center sentiment.
Pre-earnings dip buys (T1: NVDA long) fail when the market is in 'priced for perfection' mode — even a clean beat gets sold when positioning is crowded and macro headwinds (fading rate cuts) exist.
h1_closing_observations.regime_detected: AI bubble fatigue / sell-the-news / rate cut uncertainty. Not a trend-following regime for AI chips. Better suited: pairs trades (CEG long vs NNE/SMR short within nuclear), post-earnings momentum shorts on extended names.
h1_closing_observations.what_worked: Shorting speculative pre-revenue names on narrative-driven pullbacks (SMR +30.4%, NNE +13.3%). Identifying operational vs speculative nuclear differentiation. Post-gap sell-the-news on NVDA (T4 +12.8%).
h1_closing_observations.what_failed: Pre-earnings dip buy on extended AI names (T1 NVDA -5.5%). Shorting high-conviction momentum names without tight stop management (T2 PLTR -7.0%).
h1_closing_observations.h1_final_note: This is the last week of H1 backtesting (Nov 17-21, 2025). The period captured the most important event of H1: NVDA Q3 FY2026 earnings. The paradox of the best AI earnings report in history (-$80B buyback, +62% YoY revenue) causing a stock collapse is the defining signal of H1 market regime: AI bull market in transition from fundamental-driven to sentiment/positioning-driven.

price_verification.data_source: yfinance historical data, verified via python3 yf.Ticker().history()

metadata.backtesting_standard: News-first: each trade decision based on information available at entry date. No look-ahead bias on price. Exit prices verified via yfinance 20-trading-day rule.
